title: Iran Truce Wobbles, Risk Assets Slide
content: Renewed Israeli strikes on southern Lebanon and an Iranian threat to re-close the Strait of Hormuz threw the days-old US-Iran ceasefire into doubt, forcing the postponement of technical talks in Switzerland. Risk assets sold off, with bitcoin sliding around 3% to roughly $62,000 and the geopolitical risk premium creeping back into oil.
summary: Renewed Israeli strikes on Lebanon and postponed US-Iran talks revived Middle East risk, knocking crypto lower and reawakening oil's war premium.
context:
• A 14-point memorandum of understanding, signed virtually by President Trump and Vice-President Vance at the G7 in France on 15 June, had promised a 60-day ceasefire and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

• The strait carries roughly a fifth of seaborne oil and LNG, and its months-long disruption had driven Brent above $100 earlier in the year.

• Markets had rallied through mid-June on hopes the deal would permanently remove the region's energy risk premium.
happened:
• Israel said it struck more than 80 targets in southern Lebanon and the Beqaa Valley overnight, with Lebanese authorities reporting at least 18 people killed.

• Technical US-Iran talks scheduled for Bürgenstock in Switzerland were postponed, Vice-President Vance cancelled his trip, and Iran threatened to close the Strait of Hormuz again.

• Bitcoin fell around 3% to about $62,000, with ether, XRP and solana down 3-5%, as the formal signing catalyst evaporated.
implications:
• A collapse of the truce would put the war premium straight back into Brent and WTI, reviving inflation and stagflation fears just as central banks turn hawkish.

• A reported Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire from Friday offers a tentative off-ramp, but the indefinite delay to the US-Iran signing leaves the deal fragile.

• Continued instability would support safe havens — gold, the dollar and the Swiss franc — at the expense of cyclical and risk assets.
importance:
• The Strait of Hormuz remains the single biggest swing factor for global energy prices and, by extension, the inflation outlook.

• Crypto's slide shows how quickly geopolitical headlines now transmit into 24/7 risk markets when equity exchanges are shut.

• With US markets closed for Juneteenth on Friday, futures, FX and crypto were the only live gauges of how investors read the setback.

title: BoE Holds at 3.75% as Hawks Gain Ground
content: The Bank of England left Bank Rate at 3.75% on Thursday, but two of nine MPC members voted to raise it, up from a single dissenter in April, after inflation stayed above target. The hawkish split nudged short-dated gilt yields higher and underpinned sterling.
summary: The Bank of England held Bank Rate at 3.75% in a 7-2 vote, with two members pushing for a hike as inflation ran at 2.8%.
context:
• UK CPI inflation was 2.8% in May, still comfortably above the 2% target and kept sticky in part by Middle East-driven energy costs.

• The decision came in a packed 'central bank super week' alongside the Fed, BoJ and ECB.

• The Bank is also running down its balance sheet, with the asset purchase facility cut to £523bn from a £895bn peak as of 10 June.
happened:
• The MPC voted 7-2 to hold, with two members favouring a 25bp increase — a more hawkish split than April's 8-1.

• Policymakers pointed to firmer-than-expected services inflation and the lingering energy shock from the Iran conflict.

• Short-dated gilt yields edged higher and sterling held firm as traders trimmed bets on near-term cuts.
implications:
• A widening hawkish minority raises the prospect that the next move could be a hike rather than a cut if inflation fails to cool.

• Continued quantitative tightening adds to gilt supply, keeping upward pressure on UK borrowing costs.

• Sterling's path now hinges on whether UK rates can hold their premium as the Fed also turns hawkish.
importance:
• The UK is one of the few major economies where the market is debating hikes rather than cuts — a notable cross-asset signal.

• Gilt yields anchor UK mortgage and corporate borrowing costs, with direct effects on housing and equities.

• The decision sharpens the global theme of central banks leaning hawkish into sticky, energy-driven inflation.

title: Wall Street Rebounds as Chips Lead Recovery
content: US equities bounced back on Thursday, recovering the previous session's Fed-driven sell-off, with the Nasdaq up around 1.9% and the S&P 500 climbing back to about 7,500 as semiconductor and small-cap shares led. Energy stocks lagged as the US-Iran deal initially pointed to lower oil.
summary: US stocks rebounded from the post-Fed sell-off, led by chips and small caps, with the S&P 500 back near 7,500 and the Nasdaq up about 1.9%.
context:
• Equities had fallen the prior session after the Fed's hawkish projections signalled a possible 2026 rate hike.

• Mega-cap technology and AI-linked chipmakers have remained the market's core leadership through 2026's volatility.

• The mid-June US-Iran MOU had pushed oil to its lowest since early March, weighing on energy shares.
happened:
• The Nasdaq Composite rose about 1.91% to 26,517.93 and the S&P 500 gained 1.08% to 7,500.58, while the Dow added 0.14%.

• The small-cap Russell 2000 outperformed with a 2.12% advance as a modest pullback in Treasury yields aided rate-sensitive names.

• Energy was the standout laggard as the prospect of reopened Hormuz traffic pressured crude.
implications:
• Leadership concentration in chips leaves the indices vulnerable to any AI-spending or rate disappointment.

• Small-cap outperformance hints at hopes for broadening, but that depends on yields staying contained.

• The rebound's durability rests on the Iran truce holding and the Fed not delivering a hike sooner than priced.
importance:
• With the S&P 500 back near its early-June record, the tape shows how much good news is already in equity valuations.

• Sector rotation — chips up, energy down — tells investors how the market is pricing both the Fed and the Middle East.

• Small caps are a real-time barometer of risk appetite and rate expectations.

title: Fed Holds but Dots Flag a 2026 Hike
content: The Federal Reserve, under new Chair Kevin Warsh, held its policy rate at 3.50%-3.75% for a fourth straight meeting, but its updated projections showed nine of eighteen officials now expect at least one rate increase this year. Treasury yields jumped and the dollar climbed to a two-month high.
summary: The Fed held rates at 3.50%-3.75% but its dot plot flagged a possible 2026 hike, sending yields and the dollar higher.
context:
• Inflation has stayed above target through 2026, lifted by the energy shock from the Middle East conflict.

• The meeting was the centrepiece of a global 'central bank super week'.

• Markets had entered the year expecting cuts, making the hawkish pivot a sharp repricing.
happened:
• The Fed left rates unchanged but raised its median year-end rate projection to 3.8% from 3.4% in March, with six officials seeing two or more hikes.

• The 2026 median PCE inflation forecast was lifted to 3.6% from 2.7%.

• The 2-year Treasury yield rose about 15bp to 4.21% and the 10-year to 4.49%, while the dollar index hit roughly 100.7, its highest since May 2025.
implications:
• Money markets now see a hike as likely by September and fully priced by October — a major shift from rate-cut expectations.

• A higher-for-longer dollar tightens global financial conditions and pressures emerging-market and commodity currencies.

• Equity valuations face a higher discount rate just as earnings season approaches.
importance:
• The Fed sets the global cost of capital; a pivot from cuts to potential hikes resets every asset class.

• Rising yields challenge the year's equity leadership, especially long-duration technology.

• The dollar's strength is a key channel transmitting US policy to the rest of the world.

title: BoJ Lifts Rate to 1%, a 31-Year High
content: The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate by 25bp to 1.00% on Monday, the highest since 1995, in a 7-1 vote as it pressed on with policy normalisation. The move underlined a global tilt towards tighter policy, though the yen stayed under pressure against a hawkish-Fed dollar.
summary: The Bank of Japan hiked to 1.00%, a 31-year high, continuing normalisation even as a strong dollar kept the yen weak.
context:
• Japan has been gradually exiting decades of ultra-loose policy as inflation and wage growth take hold.

• At 1.00%, borrowing costs are at their highest since September 1995.

• The yen has remained weak as the Fed's rate advantage overwhelms BoJ tightening.
happened:
• The Policy Board voted 7-1 to lift the overnight call rate to 1.00%, with member Asada Toichiro dissenting on downside growth risks.

• The decision matched near-unanimous market expectations.

• The yen stayed soft against the dollar as traders weighed the hawkish Fed outlook against Japan's gradual path.
implications:
• Higher Japanese rates raise the cost of the yen carry trade, a long-running source of global liquidity.

• Rising JGB yields could pull Japanese capital home, with ripple effects across global bond markets.

• Further hikes hinge on whether wage and price gains prove durable.
importance:
• The BoJ is the last major central bank to normalise, so each hike carries outsized signalling value.

• Yen-funded carry trades underpin positioning across global markets, making BoJ shifts systemic.

• Japanese demand is pivotal for US Treasuries and other sovereign bonds.
